dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a patient with heart problems/AICD.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Right-sided chest pain that started around 5:00 AM and radiates to my right shoulder and arm.
It's constant. I tried one nitroglycerin this morning, but it didn't help.
You're alert and able to speak clearly. How would you rate your chest pain on a scale from 0 to 10?
About a 9.
Ma'am, we're going to take your blood pressure now.
Your pulse is 97. Your respiratory rate is 25.
We'll also take an EKG now to evaluate your heart rhythm.
Do you have any rashes, bruises, or moles we should note?
Any neck pain or stiffness?
How is your mouth and throat area?
We're going to listen to your heart now.
Your oxygen level is 98%. Is there anything that aggravates your chest pain?
You mentioned chest pain that didn't improve with nitroglycerin. Any other symptoms?
Do you have any allergies we should be aware of?
What medications do you currently take?
Can you tell me a bit about your health history?
When was the last time you ate or drank something?
What were you doing when the chest pain started?
We're placing leads for a 12-lead EKG now.
Got the EKG. Our first reading showed sinus rhythm with some nonspecific T wave abnormality.
I'm going to start an IV in your arm to prepare for treatment.
Another EKG shows no major issues, but we recommend close monitoring.
We'll also be monitoring the cardiovascular system during transport.
The hospital is prepared to receive you. We're heading to VCU Health Systems.
We're beginning transport. We'll help you into a comfortable position now.
Patient is transferred to the emergency department. Your belongings are left at your bedside.
Your blood sugar is currently reading as 0.
Your latest oxygen saturation is 96%.
Your EKG still shows sinus rhythm but with occasional irregularities, including ventricular premature complexes.
You're alert and oriented. Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
I have a history of major depression, hyperlipidemia, asthma, osteoarthritis, and obesity.
I take Albuterol, Aspirin, Atorvastatin (Lipitor), HCTZ (Microzide), Lisinopril (Prinivil), Loratadine (Claratine), Nitroglycerin, and Trazodone (Desyrel).
I'm allergic to sulfasalazine (Azulfidine).
We've secured an IV in your arm vein-specifically in the AC, taking two attempts but now working.
You said it's right-sided. Any changes in how it feels or where?
When exactly did the chest pain start this morning?
Is the chest pain made worse by any activity or resting?
Can you describe the feeling? Sharp, heavy, tight?
Does it ever spread beyond your right shoulder and arm?
You mentioned 9 out of 10-any changes in the pain level during the day?
Any new symptoms since the pain started?
VCU Health Systems is securing a bed for you now.
Prepare to move to the stretcher. I'll assist to avoid straining your arm.
You denied shortness of breath, dizziness or fainting. Correct?
Yes, I had chest pain only with no other symptoms.
Did anything happen the night before that could've contributed?
Your oxygen level remained steady at 96%.
We noted your latest vital signs and EKG again before transport.
Your pain level remains at 9. Any changes in the past hour?
IV established in the arm vein. We can administer fluids or meds as needed.
You're on all necessary meds. Any concerns about missing a dose?
EKG has stable sinus rhythm, just some nonspecific abnormalities.
Your second blood pressure is 128/85 with a slower pulse of 93.
Your breathing rate has slowed to 7 breaths per minute.
The decreased respiratory rate at rest shows no indication of distress at this moment.
No new interventions needed at this time. Stable vitals and pain level.
Patient remains stable and alert en route. No interventions required at this time.
Heart rhythm remains consistent with occasional premature beats-no signs of STEMI of the anterior wall.
Transport preparations complete. Let's get you up with my help.
We will take more readings in the next few minutes for baseline comparisons.
You have no concerns about the current medication regime.
Taking another 12-lead to keep updated and minimize missed findings.
Blood pressure is 128/85, slightly elevated but not concerning for triage at this stage.
Coordinating with the hospital for any updates or changes once we arrive.
Transport precautions followed. Ready for ED transition.
Patient is now in the ambulance secured and ready for transport to the hospital.
Oxygen level is now 96% and stable.
Blood sugar remains at 0-monitoring continues for any changes.
12-lead now confirms no signs of STEMI of the anterior wall.
Pain level still at 9 on the scale-remains unchanged.
No incidents during transport. Smooth movement, patient remained still and cooperative.
Maintained route to hospital is direct and swift.
All transport precautions followed. Ready for ED transition.
Arrival imminent. Hospital prepared for ED care and immediate relay of EKG and vitals.
One final read of your vitals for comprehensive reporting.
Full assessments repeated-no changes in vital signs, response, nor cardiac rhythm.
Final accounts from the morning-no apparent reason given for chest pain.
Medics preparing for handoff now at VCU Health Systems.
Transitioning to hospital staff. All findings reviewed with hospital on arrival.
You'll be further assessed in the emergency room. Thank you for cooperation.
Hospital intake confirmed. VCU Medical team alerted.
All-to-ED transition complete. EMS task completed per protocol.
All EKG steps taken were recorded and reported with time stamps.
Your IV in the extremity vein is secure for any needs in the hospital.
Patient went directly to ED Bed 22.
Belongings left at your side, per protocol.
Entire EKG procedure notes transmitted with unit report.
No calls or interruptions during transport.
Transport time checked and within expected safety margins.
We have your albuterol as used per current protocol.
Complete medication list recorded and available in EKG and EMS reports.
Per hospital and protocol, six 12-lead EKGs obtained for full review.
Provided hospital with all care steps for continuity.
All documentation sent for traceability by paramedic.
Patient remained stable throughout the transport and arrived intact.
All care, vital signs, and interventions covered in report for hospital retrieval.
Patient transported without incident and in accordance with medical protocol.
